profile: St. Hugh of Grenoble (1053-1132) was a French bishop and a key supporter of the Carthusian Order. Though he felt inadequate for his role and often requested to resign, Pope Gregory VII insisted he remain as bishop. He was known for his eloquent preaching, his reform of his diocese, and for granting St. Bruno the land where the Grande Chartreuse, the motherhouse of the Carthusian Order, was built.

Holy Thursday commemorates the Last Supper of Jesus Christ with the Apostles, the institution of the Eucharist, the institution of the priesthood, and Christ's command of brotherly love through the washing of feet. It marks the beginning of the Easter Triduum, the three most sacred days of the Catholic liturgical year.

saintName: Good Friday of the Lord's Passion
profile: Good Friday is the solemn memorial of the crucifixion and death of Jesus Christ at Calvary. It is a day of strict fasting, penance, and mourning, where the Church strips her altars and venerates the Cross, remembering the supreme sacrifice of the Lamb of God for the redemption of humanity.

Holy Saturday commemorates the day Jesus Christ lay in the tomb after his death. The Church waits in silence and fasting until nightfall, when the Easter Vigil is celebrated. This magnificent liturgy begins in darkness, kindles the new fire, blesses the Paschal candle, and welcomes new members into the Church through Baptism and Confirmation.

saintName: Easter Sunday of the Resurrection of the Lord
profile: Easter Sunday is the greatest feast in the Christian calendar, celebrating the glorious resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead on the third day after his crucifixion. It is the triumph of life over death, the fulfillment of Old Testament prophecies, and the foundation of the Christian faith and hope for eternal life.

St. Crescentia Hoess (1682-1744) was a German Franciscan Tertiary who endured immense hardship and rejection when she first entered the convent because her family was too poor to provide a dowry. She responded to cruelty with profound humility and cheerful service, eventually becoming novice mistress and superior, known for her mystical visions and deep spiritual wisdom.

saintName: St. John Baptist de La Salle
profile: St. John Baptist de La Salle (1651-1719) was a French priest and educational reformer who founded the Institute of the Brothers of the Christian Schools (De La Salle Brothers). He revolutionized education by teaching in the vernacular rather than Latin, grouping students by ability, and making high-quality education accessible to the poor. He is the patron saint of teachers.

St. Julie Billiart (1751-1816) was a French woman who, despite being paralyzed for 22 years and surviving the terror of the French Revolution, founded the Sisters of Notre Dame de Namur to educate young girls. She was miraculously cured of her paralysis and spent the rest of her life tirelessly expanding her order, always repeating her motto: 'How good is the good God.'

St. Casilda (d. 1050) was the daughter of a Muslim leader in Toledo, Spain, during the Islamic occupation. Secretly sympathetic to the Christian prisoners, she smuggled food to them in her skirts. When her father demanded to see what she was hiding, the bread miraculously turned into roses. She eventually converted to Christianity and lived as a solitary ascetic.

St. Magdalene of Canossa (1774-1835) was an Italian noblewoman who renounced her immense wealth and status to serve the poor. Seeing the devastating effects of poverty and ignorance in Verona, she founded the Canossian Daughters of Charity, dedicating her life to education, healthcare, and pastoral work for abandoned children and marginalized women.

St. Stanislaus (1030-1079) was the Bishop of Kraków and a fierce defender of moral integrity against the corrupt King Bolesław II the Bold. Excommunicating the king for his scandalous and cruel behavior, Stanislaus was martyred when the king personally murdered him while the bishop was celebrating Mass. He is a patron saint of Poland.

Divine Mercy Sunday, celebrated on the Octave Day of Easter, highlights the unfathomable mercy of God as revealed through the private revelations to St. Faustina Kowalska. Instituted by St. John Paul II, the feast invites the faithful to trust entirely in Christ's redeeming love, offering special graces of complete forgiveness of sins and punishment to those who receive Holy Communion and go to Confession.

St. Martin I (d. 655) was a courageous Pope who vigorously defended the orthodox teaching that Jesus Christ possessed both a human and divine will, condemning the Monothelite heresy. Because of his steadfast faith, he was arrested by the Byzantine Emperor, subjected to brutal imprisonment, and died in exile, making him the last Pope to be honored as a martyr.

Blessed Peter Gonzalez (1190-1246) was a Spanish Dominican friar who, after a public humiliation riding a horse, abandoned his vain pursuit of worldly honors to embrace a life of severe penance and preaching. He became the confessor to King St. Ferdinand III, but dedicated most of his time preaching to sailors and the poor, becoming the patron saint of Spanish mariners.

St. Caesar de Bus (1544-1607) was a French priest who transitioned from a life of military and courtly leisure to profound piety. Recognizing the dire need for religious education following the Council of Trent, he founded the Fathers of Christian Doctrine (Doctrinarians), dedicating his life to innovative, engaging, and accessible catechesis for the common people.

St. Bernadette Soubirous (1844-1879) was a humble, uneducated peasant girl from Lourdes, France, who received eighteen visions of the Blessed Virgin Mary in 1858. Despite relentless interrogation and public skepticism, she remained steadfast in conveying Mary's message of prayer and penance. She later retreated from the public eye to live as a Sister of Charity, embracing a life of quiet suffering.

St. Benedict Joseph Labre (1748-1783) was a French mendicant who, after being turned away from several strict monastic orders, found his calling as a homeless pilgrim. Traveling on foot to the great shrines of Europe, he embraced radical poverty, living off alms and spending hours in Eucharistic adoration. He died in Rome, revered by the poor as the 'saint of the Forty Hours.'

Blessed James Oldo (1364-1404) was a wealthy Italian layman whose life was transformed when he witnessed a traveling preacher and reflected on his own mortality during an outbreak of plague. He and his wife embraced the Franciscan Third Order, turning their lavish home into a chapel and dedicating their wealth to the sick and imprisoned.

St. Pope Leo IX (1002-1054) was a German aristocratic pontiff who initiated rigorous reforms within the Church, vigorously fighting against simony and clerical marriage. Through his extensive travels and synods across Europe, he reasserted papal authority. His papacy was historically significant as it coincided with the tragic onset of the Great Schism of 1054 between the Eastern and Western Churches.

St. Agnes of Montepulciano (1268-1317) was an Italian Dominican nun and mystic renowned for her profound holiness from a very young age, becoming a superior at just 15 years old. She was famous for her deep prayer life, miracles, and the physical manifestation of manna falling like snow upon her during her prayers. Her incorrupt body was later venerated by St. Catherine of Siena.

St. Anselm (1033-1109) was a Benedictine monk, Archbishop of Canterbury, and brilliant philosopher. He is considered the father of Scholasticism and is famous for his ontological argument for the existence of God. As Archbishop, he fiercely defended the independence of the Church against English kings, resulting in his exile. He is honored as a Doctor of the Church.

St. Pope Caius (d. 296) led the early Church during a rare period of relative peace before the severe persecutions of Diocletian. According to tradition, he decreed that a man must pass through all the lower orders (porter, lector, acolyte, exorcist, subdeacon, deacon, and priest) before being ordained a bishop. He is honored as a martyr of the early Church.

St. George (d. 303) was a Roman soldier of Greek origin who was martyred during the Diocletianic Persecution for refusing to renounce his Christian faith. Though the famous legend of him slaying a dragon emerged centuries later, the historical reality of his courageous martyrdom made him an enduring symbol of Christian valor and the patron saint of England and many other nations.

St. Fidelis of Sigmaringen (1577-1622) began his career as a lawyer dedicated to the poor but left the profession out of disgust with its corruption. He became a Capuchin Franciscan priest, known for his ardent preaching and tireless charity. Sent as a missionary to Calvinist Switzerland amid intense hostility, he was ambushed and martyred for defending the Catholic faith.

St. Mark the Evangelist is the traditional author of the Gospel of Mark. A companion of both St. Paul and St. Peter, his Gospel is heavily influenced by Peter's eyewitness testimony. According to tradition, Mark founded the Church in Alexandria, Egypt, becoming its first bishop before being martyred. His symbol in Christian art is the winged lion.

Our Lady of Good Counsel is a title of the Blessed Virgin Mary associated with a miraculous fresco found in the thirteenth-century Augustinian church in Genazzano, Italy. The devotion emphasizes Mary's role as a wise and loving mother who provides spiritual guidance and counsel to her children who seek her intercession in times of doubt or difficulty.

St. Zita (1212-1272) was an Italian woman who worked as a domestic servant for the wealthy Fatinelli family in Lucca from the age of twelve until her death. Initially despised by the other servants for her piety and diligence, her patient endurance and profound charity eventually won over the entire household. She is venerated as the patron saint of domestic workers.

St. Peter Chanel (1803-1841) was a French Marist priest and the first martyr of Oceania. Sent as a missionary to the island of Futuna, he struggled for years with little success in converting the local populace. However, after he baptized the chieftain's son, the enraged chieftain had him clubbed to death. His martyrdom sparked a massive wave of conversions across the island.

St. Catherine of Siena (1347-1380) was a Dominican tertiary and mystic whose profound union with Christ propelled her into the center of medieval European politics. Though unlettered, she dictated 'The Dialogue,' a masterpiece of spiritual theology, and famously convinced Pope Gregory XI to return the papacy from Avignon to Rome. She is a Doctor of the Church and co-patroness of Europe.

St. Pope Pius V (1504-1572) was a Dominican friar who, as Pope, vigorously implemented the sweeping reforms of the Council of Trent. He standardized the Roman Missal, published the Roman Catechism, and organized the Holy League that defeated the Ottoman fleet at the miraculous Battle of Lepanto in 1571, an event he attributed to the intercession of the Rosary.
